metadata.title: Can end‐user feedback inform ‘Responsibilisation’ of India's policy landscape for agri‐digital transition?*

abstract: Agrarian transformations are imagined globally through digital agriculture. Most ambitious agrarian plans in India also identify the application of digital technologies to drive the next agro-food revolution. However, the concerns around the 'soft impacts' of the agri-digital transition, including socioethical concerns and farmers' perceptions, have received relatively little attention among innovators and policymakers. Thus, through a combination of qualitative content analysis and topic modelling, this article studies end-user feedback on smart farming apps in India. Emerging themes from the feedback analysis inform an understanding of the attributes of responsibilisation in policies through which agri-digitalisation is being planned and introduced. The main findings indicate that agri-digital innovations need to be inclusive of nonhegemonic vernaculars, responsive to farmers' data privacy and ownership considerations and focus on producing simple, actionable insights for farmers instead of bombarding them with conflicting ideas. At the policy level, this article argues that the Indian policy landscape needs to be reformed substantially. This further suggests that India may need a new institutional architecture to manage the digital transition in agriculture.

paragraphs: Digital technologies have emerged as a significant configuration in the development of agriculture. Innovations like smart farming apps, farming as a service platform and farm management information systems enable access to farming advice, market information and supply chain services, thus facilitating productive, sustainable and less labour-intensive agriculture (Basso & Antle, 2020;Lio & Liu, 2006;Rose & Chilvers, 2018;Sunding & Zilberman, 2001). Disembodied interfaces, such as smartphones, often provide the technological front end for embodied, precision-farming technologies, including robots, drones and sensors. For instance, sensors attached to a tractor send farm data to the cloud, enabling farmers to make decisions by accessing that data through smart farming apps. These digital technologies offer opportunities not only for large-scale agriculture in industrialised countries, but also for smallholders in developing countries, where disembodied innovations can become crucial for agricultural transformation (Aceto et al., 2019;Birner et al., 2021;CTA, 2019;Ehlers et al., 2021;Kamble et al., 2020;Poppe et al., 2013;Walter et al., 2017) However, various studies in the developed world have warned against an unfettered rollingout of this 'techno-utopian' (Dickel & Schrape, 2017) digitalisation program (Aker et al., 2016). Among the arguments made are that the digitalisation of agriculture may disrupt the farming space (social as well as policy; Carolan, 2018;Eastwood et al., 2019), subjugate people and planet (Bronson & Knezevic, 2016) and make the food security narrative too technocratic, thus sidelining a straightforward 'social response narrative' (Klerkx & Rose, 2020). While this critique is now quite well-stimulated in advanced countries, a critical unpacking of digital agriculture is largely missing in the developing world context, except for a few recent works (e.g., Brooks, 2021;Chamuah & Singh, 2019).

The aim of this article is to help fill this gap by considering the agri-digitalisation case of India. India is moving rapidly towards digital governance through several schemes, such as the Digital India project and Aadhar. 1 Agri-digitisation is a crucial focus agenda for various institutional actors, including the Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare (MAFW), whose Dalwai committee report on Doubling Farmers' Income represents digital technologies as a 'science that optimises on the inputs, helps to reduce costs, [and] makes the production more competitive' (MAFW, 2018b, p. v).

Smart farming apps are the prime focus of India's (still formative) approach to agri-digital policy. The government is launching different apps to facilitate things like advice on prices and crop insurance, while entrepreneurs are being encouraged to build apps that resolve the bottlenecks in the farm-to-fork spectrum. Such smart farming tools provide a distinct sense of scale neutrality (Carolan, 2020) that can have transformational utility on small farms at a very small investment (Lajoie-O' Malley et al., 2020). They can be as useful on the small and marginal farms of Bihar (of which approximately two-thirds have operational landholdings of under 2 hectares) as on the large farms of Punjab (with two-thirds of landholdings larger than 4 hectares). Overall, India's high mobile penetration (EY, 2020) and low average farm size (MAFW, 2020) make smart farming apps significant for agriculture.

As a facilitator of value-creating interaction between producers and consumers of agriculture (Cole & Fernando, 2012;Inwood & Dale, 2019), farming apps provide an opportunity to identify emerging techno-social concerns, anticipated risks and tech-(in)compatibility issues through the process of end-user feedback analysis. 2 This assumes a holistic approach in which technology-led development occurs in a responsible way.

For the end-user feedback analysis in this article, we have applied insights from the Responsible Research and Innovation (RRI) framework (Fleming et al., 2021;Owen, 2019) to identify inadequacies in existing policies and ways that they can be reformed to responsiblise the agri-digital transition. The application of RRI enables an earlier anticipation of issues with the agri-digital transition, which is needed to shape a desirable outcome. Otherwise, there are risks of negative consequences from technological innovation-of which there are ample examples from around the world (Basu & Leeuwis, 2012;Harry and Cleaver, 1972;Collingridge, 1982;Fleming et al., 2021;Gathorne-Hardy et al., 2016;Herring, 2014;Ruivenkamp, 1993).

Thus, building from an empirical foundation of end-user feedback, this article advocates a responsibilisation of the ways that agri-digitalisation is being planned in India by identifying and analysing emerging policy-specific concerns. Methodologically, it studies the impending digital transition of Indian agriculture through a fusion of machine-learning techniques with qualitative content analysis (i.e., of end-user feedback and policy texts). In terms of structure, the following sections briefly review the global transition towards digital agriculture and explain end-user feedback and RRI, the methodology, the results with policy implications are discussed and the conclusion.

sections.header_raw: RESPONSIBLISATION AND THE TRANSITION TOWARDS DIGITAL AGRICULTURE
sections.header_norm: responsiblisation and the transition towards digital agriculture
paragraphs: Many countries in the world are promoting agri-digitalisation as a central instrument for agriculture policy, such as Horizon 2020 and Smart agricultural knowledge and innovation systems (AKIS) in the EU, the Cornell Open Ag Initiative in the United States and, in India, national e-governance (EIS, 2018;Klerkx et al., 2019;MAFW, 2016a). Among many emerging technologies, digital agriculture leads technology's readiness-for-implementation chart and is being widely adopted across the global north and global south (Herrero et al., 2020). Techno-optimistic narratives coming from multilateral institutions (the World Bank, Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO), Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD), etc.) envision agridigitalisation as an inevitable transition for both large and small farmers (Lajoie-O' Malley et al., 2020).

In the literature, hypernyms such as 'Agriculture 4.0' (Frankelius et al., 2017), 'Fourth agriculture revolution' (Rose & Chilvers, 2018) and 'Agri-Food 4.0' (Miranda et al., 2019) are applied. These are used interchangeably to denote both the application of software solutions, big data and artificial intelligence (AI) to agriculture and the idea and process of digital agriculture and agridigitalisation more broadly. There are also conflicting stories behind the numeric suffixation with an alternative contextualisation of '4.0' (Miranda et al., 2019;Rose & Chilvers, 2018). However, there is a consensus that the digital transition of the age of 4.0 will revolutionise human-machineland interaction.

The manifest and ubiquitous computational advantages in managing data (high volume, velocity, veracity, variety, value) and converting it into actionable insights using emerging technologies such as the Internet of Things (IoT), blockchain and AI make digital agriculture quite distinct from its predecessors, such as precision farming (Basso & Antle, 2020). Evidence shows that agridigitalisation is, indeed, increasing productivity (Lio & Liu, 2006), redesigning landscapes for sustainable cropping (Basso & Antle, 2020) and reducing work-related fatigue (Regan, 2019;Rose & Chilvers, 2018).

Evidence from developing economies also affirms the role of digital technologies in effective production management. This is achieved through digitised extension services, more effective supply chain logistics and better price realisation (Carolan, 2020;Daum et al., 2021;Deichmann et al., 2016;EY, 2020). For instance, Nigeria's Hello Tractor and India's EM3 facilitate hassle-free digital booking for tractors and Global Positioning System (GPS)-aided monitoring to track location, fuel efficiency and farming activities (Daum et al., 2021). In India, technologies like CropIn enable accurate, data-driven insights for farm businesses; Plantix provides app-based disease detection, and Intello Labs and Agricx support AI-enabled crop yield and quality monitoring, while actors like SourceTrace and DeHaat are addressing issues of waiting time, transparency, traceability and price discovery in the farm-to-fork supply chain (EY, 2020). Smart farming apps play the role of front-end technology for all of these.

However, these optimistic social (people), economic (production/profit and ecological (planet) future 'imaginings' (Wright et al., 2013) lack robust scrutiny. First, in policy discourse and among innovators, they are lopsided, being biased towards a production (profit) focus in which people and the planet are little considered or simply ignored (Barrett & Rose, 2022;Rose et al., 2021b). Then, and relatedly, the value of digital agriculture and its benefit distribution among stakeholders on the supply side (innovators and investors) and demand side (farmers) is problematic. As Fraser (2018) notes, farm data-collected, processed, analysed and repackaged in the form of insightsare becoming a new type of cash crop, one whose larger benefits become appropriated by technology firms. Thus, instead of bringing inclusive benefits to all (people, production/profit, planet), digital agriculture only reinforces the existing power structures (Cowie et al., 2020;Fraser, 2021). Given this developmental framing, it is not so surprising that the actual lived experience of farmers adopting digital agriculture often fails to meet their expectations (Kernecker et al., 2019;Rijswijk et al., 2019).

Scholars have raised concerns regarding data privacy and security (e.g., privacy trade-offs in the usage of digital apps) and emergent conflicts in digital technology's intellectual property ownership and access (Carolan, 2018(Carolan, , 2019;;Wolfert et al., 2017). These risks include the imminent 'Orwellian dangers' of 'surveillance capitalism' (Zuboff, 2015) as well as 'Kafkaesque dangers' in leaks, lapses and commercial misappropriation of farmers' data (Solove, 2004). Also, in terms of decision-making, as the sensor (automated) takes over from common sense (human), the possibility of various disconnects becomes significant, notably between farmers and 'their' technology (Regan, 2019). Data and algorithm-driven insights may be expected to disrupt the farmer's heuristic and intuition-based decision-making, for example, raising trust concerns.

Therefore, it becomes critical to understand agri-digital technologies as a 'sociocyber-physical' bundle and inform the innovation process with 'responsibilisation' attributes (Rijswijk et al., 2021). The aim of this is to purposefully identify emerging (in)compatibility issues and socioethical concerns, such as the blocking or lock-out of end users from development and hence of smallholders from substantial gains.

Among many emerging technologies, digital agriculture leads technology's readiness-for-implementation chart and is being widely adopted across the global north and global south (Herrero et al., 2020). Techno-optimistic narratives coming from multilateral institutions (the World Bank, Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO), Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD), etc.) envision agridigitalisation as an inevitable transition for both large and small farmers (Lajoie-O' Malley et al., 2020).

sections.paper_id: Kumar and Basu - 2022 - Can end‐user feedback inform ‘Responsibilisation’ of India's policy landscape for agri‐digital trans
paragraphs: Responsibilisation calls for a consideration of the socioeconomic and ethical aspects intertwined within technological artefacts of innovation. It involves concerned stakeholders, inviting them to identify and reflect upon the intended and unintended consequences of innovation and thereby contribute to a recalibration of the innovation landscape (Maye et al., 2019). To this end, the RRI framework may be employed, which comprises four dimensions: anticipation, inclusion, reflexivity and responsiveness (AIRR; Macnaghten, 2016;Owen et al., 2013;Stilgoe et al., 2013).

Here, AIRR can be understood as (1) identifying unintended risks of innovation and considering contingency (anticipation); (2) facilitating possibilities for the consideration of new voices in order to benefit from the input of all stakeholder views in the governance of innovation (inclusion); (3) acknowledging institutional limitations and appreciating that one particular framing may not be universally held, thus constantly monitoring the innovation process to keep it open to different perspectives (reflexivity); and (4) promoting the creative capacity of science policy institutions to shape directions of innovation in response to issues raised so as to align innovation with societal values (responsiveness).

A few studies have applied RRI in agro-food transition. These have included analyses of emerging socioethical dilemmas in New Zealand's smart dairy farming (Eastwood et al., 2019); the knock-on effects of technology on farmers' identity and exclusion of old and smaller farmers from digital agriculture imaginings in Ireland (Regan, 2019); and the different views of innovation communities on Australia's Digitalisation of Agricultural Innovation System (Fielke et al., 2019).

As previous studies assert, there are various benefits from a user-centered approach stimulating end-user feedback (Basu, 2016;Basu et al., 2017aBasu et al., , 2017bBasu et al., , 2018)). For example, it helps to reduce the 'design-reality gaps' and scale the technology in the long run (Lynch & Gregor, 2004;Steinke et al., 2020). Recently, Ortiz-Crespo et al. (2020) showed that a user-centered design enhances the adoption and efficiency of phone-based agri-extension services in Tanzania. From the RRI perspective, end-user feedback provides a 'solid understanding of people in their social context' (Diederiks & Hoonhout, 2007, p. 32). End-user feedback can also be methodologically mapped to some of the potential responsible innovation activities proposed by Eastwood et al. (2019), such as assessing farmer perceptions of technology and seeking critical feedback from stakeholders. Concerns thus emerging can guide the anticipation of unintended risks, compatibility issues and socioethical implications to make the innovation process more reflexive and responsive (Eastwood et al., 2019;Rose, 2021a).

sections.paper_id: Kumar and Basu - 2022 - Can end‐user feedback inform ‘Responsibilisation’ of India's policy landscape for agri‐digital trans
paragraphs: This research applies a mixed-method approach analysing end-user (farmer) feedback and policy documentation. First, we investigate the corpus of policy texts using a qualitative content analysis to identify significant timelines and events, key policy actors and critical technologies. Then, in the Results of the End-User Feedback Analysis section, we analyse farmer feedback on the usage of smart farming apps. This provides the key emergent themes that are reflected in the Emerging Themes for Policy Reflection section through a thematic analysis of the policy texts (Figure 1).

sections.container_raw: F I G U R E 1 Methodological schema of the end-user feedback and policy analysis
sections.container_norm: f i g u r e 1 methodological schema of the end user feedback and policy analysis
paragraphs: In contrast to traditional methods of analysing farmers' feedback through surveys, interviews and focus group discussions, this article uses smart farming app review data (extracted from the Google Play store) for farmers' opinions as expressed and stored in a digital repository. The app review dataset affords the flexibility to extract farmers' views for a longer time scale (here, we scraped reviews from January 2014 to July 2020). Although the extraction and collation of farmers' review data involve some computationally implemented procedures, it gives a better trade-off between cost, time and data collection performance than traditional methods (Guo et al., 2017).

These reviews-a hotspot of valuable information (McIlroy et al., 2015)-reflect upon the sentiments of smart farming app users in the form of experience stories, feedback and complaints. This dataset does have some limitations, though. First, it does not support any analysis involving farmer demographics and farm size characterisation. Thus, the scope of the article is limited to discovering hidden semantic structures in the review text corpus and reflecting upon the same for the inclusion of end-user feedback into responsible policy pathways. Second, it may contain meaningless spam or irrelevant opinions (Fang & Zhan, 2015). Thus, we performed two stages of filtering to sort out quality discrepancies by removing insignificant and uninformative data.

We shortlisted eight smart farming apps (developed by agri-tech startups) providing advisory and other services (see Table 1) based on inclusion criteria of 100,000+ downloads and a minimum 4.1-star rating as of 30 July 2020. We also included two representative government apps-e-NAM (a digital platform for farmers to commercialise their produce through pan-India electronic trading) and Kisan Suvidha (a primary advisory and information service app). Table 1 gives a summary of the services provided by the selected apps.

We scraped a total of 27,397 farmer reviews (as of 30 July 2020) from the Google Play store that were cleaned and preprocessed for exploratory analysis. Small string reviews were found to be insignificant (see Table 2). Thus, we removed all reviews with a text length of fewer than six words. This gave us a corpus of 7,281 reviews as the initial dataset for analysis. We proceeded with two types of analysis on the initial dataset.

We applied latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) topic modelling (Blei et al., 2003). This is one of the emerging machine-learning models for content analysis. 3 Topic modelling ensures a sense of the researcher's value-neutrality (Hagen, 2018) as topical cohorts are formed under the unsupervised learning paradigm. The basic assumption is that texts can be classified into a set of topics. The LDA topic model identifies these based on the keywords that occur in one topical cohort and not in others (Jacobi et al., 2016). The model gives the output as a set of keyword clusters, with each cluster forming a topical cohort onto which texts can be probabilistically mapped. The interpretation of topical cohorts is informed by the domain knowledge of the researcher and their understanding gained from sample reviews in each cohort. The main methodological steps in LDA topic modelling involve the following:

1. text data collection (scraped here using Google Play Scraper API [google-play-scraper 0.1.1, n.d.]); 2. data anonymisation (anonymising all identity variables [i.e., name and unique review ID] and creating a new randomised review ID); 3. cleaning (removing blank reviews, punctuations, emojis, URLs, stop words, etc.); 4. text tokenisation (splitting text sentences into word tokens), lemmatisation (converting words into their bases [lemma]) and bag of word representation (converting the text into a matrix showing the frequency of each word) followed by LDA topic modelling and interpretation of topical cohorts (Jacobi et al., 2016). All steps were carried out in the Python environment.

We validated and reaffirmed the findings from the LDA topic modelling with a qualitative content analysis on informative reviews. The corpus of 7,281 reviews was qualitatively classified as uninformative (4,857) and informative (2,424) according to relevance (Table 2). Taking an initial hint for category from the LDA model's emerging topics, the informative reviews were qualitatively coded and tagged in order to systematically analyse and interpret the farmer feedback.

Plantix is a Berlin-based app that applies an artificial neural network algorithm for real-time disease diagnosis using plant pictures; 80% of the farmers using this app are based in India; only India-based farmer reviews are included in the dataset used here.

The download criteria of 100,000+ downloads as of 30 July 2020 was relaxed for the Smartfarm app, as the developer, Cropin Technologies, uses a business-to-business model, making the app inaccessible to individual farmers.

If you make this app in local languages, the farmers will benefit much'

Based on an in-depth analysis of the different types of feedback, we identified key emerging themes with policy implications. These themes help us reflect upon policy texts to investigate and thence unravel emergent fault lines in India's policy imaginings.

The app review dataset affords the flexibility to extract farmers' views for a longer time scale (here, we scraped reviews from January 2014 to July 2020). Although the extraction and collation of farmers' review data involve some computationally implemented procedures, it gives a better trade-off between cost, time and data collection performance than traditional methods (Guo et al., 2017).

sections.header_raw: Evolution of the digital agriculture policy landscape in India
sections.header_norm: evolution of the digital agriculture policy landscape in india
paragraphs: In addition to MAFW, the key policy actors involved in the digital agriculture transition in India are the Indian Council of Agriculture Research (ICAR), the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology, Startup India and the National Institute for Transforming India (NITI Aayog; Table 3).

A discussion around agri-digitalisation was originally initiated by ICAR's (2015) Vision 2050. This document envisioned a future contingent upon an increasing convergence of bio, nano and info technologies. Without using the term 'digital agriculture' as such, the document asserted that automation configured approximately 4G-based Geographic Information System (GIS), GPS and Information and Communications Technology (ICT) would be key to unlocking planet-friendly growth trajectories. Later, NeGP 2.0 introduced the concept of digital agriculture.

In March 2015, the cabinet approved Agriculture 2.0 (digital agriculture) as a separate mission mode project under NeGP 2.0. Following this, in March 2016, the MAFW published an implementation approach article for digital agriculture. The suffix '2.0' posits digital agriculture as the successor to ICT in agriculture projects initiated in NeGP 1.0, where the focus was on information extension. Consequently, the Information Technology Division in MAFW was renamed the 'Digital Agriculture Division' in July 2020.

In NeGP 1.0, agriculture was part of the state's mission mode project. With a set of objectives including the efficient implementation of ICT in information delivery, effective government service delivery to farmers and the monitoring of agriculture projects, NeGP 1.0 envisioned 'an environment conducive for raising the farm productivity and income to global levels through provision of relevant information and services to the stakeholders' (MeitY, 2011, p. 161). Extending the above vision, Agriculture 2.0 (digital agriculture) was made a separate mission mode project under NeGP

TA B L E 3 Policy institutions and key actions Policy institution Description Digital agriculture transition through policy actions/initiatives Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers welfare (MAFW) Apex body to drive agriculture policies, laws and regulations -Implementation approach paper for digital agriculture (released in 2016) -Draft mobile app policy for agriculture (released in 2016) -Doubling Farmers' Income report (released in 2018) -Creation of Digital Agriculture Division in 2020 Indian Council of Agriculture Research Autonomous organisation under MAFW for coordination of agriculture research and education in India -Setting future vision towards digitisation: Vision 2050 document (released in 2015) -Artificial Intelligence in agriculture workshop (2018) Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology Apex body to promote e-governance and e-innovation in different fields; also assists other ministries in promotion of digital transition -National e-Governance Plan (NeGP): Digital agriculture as a mission mode project under NeGP 2.0 (released in 2015) Startup India Ministry of Commerce and Industry initiative to promote entrepreneurship ecosystem in India -Agriculture Grand Challenge (initiated in 2017) National Institute for Transforming India Government of India policy think tank (replaced Planning Commission) -National Strategy for Artificial Intelligence (2018)

2.0. The term 'Agriculture 2.0' also appears in the Dalwai committee report on doubling farmers' incomes (MAFW, 2018a). A little confusingly, the term 'Agriculture 2.0' has been used in India's policy texts to denote digital transitions, although the standard literature and policy texts emerging from global institutions use 'Agriculture 4.0' (or similar) for this, where 'Agriculture 1.0' refers to the transition from hunter-gatherers to settlement in agriculture, 'Agriculture 2.0' denotes the use of machinery (such as seed drills) instead of human labour, and 'Agriculture 3.0' refers to intensive production (such as the Green Revolution; Klerkx & Rose, 2020;Parra-López et al., 2021;Rose & Chilvers, 2018). Two plausible explanations can be given for the Indian divergence. One is that since digital agriculture as a mission mode project was incepted for the first time in NeGP 2.0, the term 'Agriculture 2.0' was used for digital agriculture as a successor to previous agricultural projects under NeGP 1.0. The other is that the term 'Agriculture 2.0' may also be construed upon India's postcolonial agrarian transitions. India entered the first set of perceptible technological shifts in agricultural practices through the green revolution during the 1960s and 1970s (Parayil, 1992). Thus, if we take this as the first transition from a traditional subsistence peasantry to modern productivism, so agriculture 1.0 in the Indian context, then the digital agriculture transition may be construed as the second or Agriculture 2.0.

The primary forms and configurations illustrated in Agriculture 2.0 (digital agriculture) were smart farming apps and portals for three thrust areas, namely, real-time price information, online input ordering and mobile banking for loans, relief and cash payments. In the 2016 digital agriculture approach article, the MAFW set the ambitious target of developing 2,000 smart-farming apps in the following three years through multiple channels, such as government, academia and crowdsourcing (MAFW, 2016a).

Information facilitation provides the core narrative of the initial forms and configurations of digital agriculture in India. As enumerated in the preamble of the agriculture mobile app policy launched in June 2016, a reliable information flow to farmers can reduce production loss, lead to better price discovery and open avenues to higher profits (MAFW, 2016b). The focus was placed on developing apps for agriculture, animal husbandry, dairy and fisheries to provide alternative information extension channels. The mobile app policy also allowed for the inclusion of private players in app development through open tendering or solution-oriented open challenge programs that incentivised emerging agri-tech entrepreneurs to scale their innovations.

The years 2015-2016 saw a more profound agri-digitalisation initiative in India. Along with the policy focus on smart farming apps and portals, institutions like ICAR started new collaborative projects in technologies such as drone technology and sensors. In July 2016, in answer to a parliament question on drone-based agriculture technology, the MAFW informed parliament about ICAR's multi-institutional collaborative project named 'SENSAGRI: SENsor based Smart AGRIculture' for a drone-based crop and soil health monitoring system using hyperspectral remote sensing sensors (Loksabha, 2016). In December 2017, the MAFW collaborated with Startup India to launch the Agriculture Grand Challenge to screen, mentor and incubate support for a few unique innovations (Startup India, n.d.). Among the ideas and projects to emerge from this were sensor-based soil testing, quick grading of farm produce, blockchain-based supply chain optimisation, yield mapping with satellite data and drone technology to minimise the cost of pesticide spray (Invest India, 2019). In order to investigate the possibilities of AI application in agriculture, ICAR organised a workshop in July 2018. This showcased diverse stakeholder engagement involving delegates from industry (IBM, TCS, Oracle, NVIDIA, etc.), academia and government (ICAR, 2018).

To sum up, while embodied technologies like drones, robotics and smart sensors are currently at a formative stage of innovation in India, the focus on smart farming apps has deepened over the last three years. ICAR-linked research institutions have launched more than 100 apps for crop advice, farming education, marketing and supply chain solutions (Mobile Apps/ICAR, n.d.). The MAFW has been instrumental in scaling up apps, such as Kisan Suvidha and e-NAM. The Mahalanobis National Crop Forecast Center (an attached office under the MAFW) has collaborated with the Indian Space Research Organisation to develop the ground truth and crop cutting experiment apps in order to collect and assess real-time field data (Digital Agriculture Division/Mo A&FW/GoI, n.d.). These apps enable policymakers to conduct agriculture surveys and manage crop insurance schemes.

Manifestly, smart farming apps are mushrooming in India as a disembodied digital approach to existing farm-to-fork concerns. Policy institutions and emerging startups in the ag-tech ecosystem are relying on apps for agri-digitisation. However, do these apps meet needs and fulfil their purpose? Are they compatible-socioethically, as well as technologically-with farmers' requirements and belief systems?

paragraphs: In reporting the results of the end-user feedback analysis, first, the topics per LDA topic modelling are given. A total of 18 topic cohorts are identified, along with their 10 most probable keywords of occurrence. Then, there follows a qualitative analysis of the informative reviews aimed at exploring farmers' perceptions of the various benefits, challenges and other compatibility factors.

paragraphs: LDA topic modelling categorises reviewed texts by keywords, thus creating cohorts of similar thematic discussions. The LDA model with 18 cohorts was determined as the best fit since the marginal increment in coherence score was at its maximum in this case. Table 4 shows the keyword cluster and interpretations for each topical cohort.

Eight of the topical cohorts were uninformative (Cohorts 2,7,8,9,10,14,15 and 17). The keyword clusters in these cohorts suggest that these reviews mostly gave strong appreciation or criticism without any specifics.
We pooled the remaining 10 informative topical cohorts into six types of dominant topics:

1. Knowledge, information and ideas (or lack of informational integrity) that lead to better (or confused) decision-making (Cohorts 5, 11, 18). 2. Advanced app services and glitches (Cohorts 5, 6, 13). 3. Function support and interface issues (or appraisal; Cohorts 3, 6). 4. Farmers' requests for new features/requirements (Cohorts 4, 12). 5. Need for diversification in crop choices facilitated by app (Cohort 16). 6. Inclusion of language and location (or lack of it; Cohorts 1, 12).

Following the lead provided by these authors, we performed a detailed qualitative coding of the review corpus. This assisted in the understanding of objective realities by exploring the detailed semantic discussions around the majority of topics and discovering essential latent topics.

paragraphs: A qualitative analysis was performed on the farmers' review corpus categorised as informative. Four types of reviews emerge from this corpus:

1. Positive appraisal: Farmers expressed the benefits of smart farming apps; 2. tech-inadequacies (or technical compatibility concerns): Farmers complained that apps were not meeting their expectations due to technical inadequacies; 3. landscape issues: Apps did not meet farmers' landscape requirements (e.g., app services were not available in their agro-climatic location or for their specific plants, vegetables and fruits); 4. social compatibility concerns: Farmers expressed a lack of socioethical sensitivity in technology design.

Almost half (44%) of the reviews fell under the first category listed here as 'positive appraisal', suggesting that smart farming apps provide a significant tech edge to farmers for better decisionmaking and help them increase yields and farm income. TA B L E 5 Thematic clustering of informative reviews Type Subtype Percentage (%) * Frequency Positive appraisal [1] Knowledge, information and ideas 24.5 594 [2] Advanced information services 16.6 402 [3] Yield, income and cost 1.7 42 Tech-inadequacies [4] Advanced info. service glitches 7.2 175 [5] Function, support and interface 12.1 294 [6] Feature requests 6.2 151 Landscape [7] Location 4.6 111 [8] Crop diversification 13.0 315 Social compatibility concerns [9] Linguistic inclusiveness 10.8 262 [10] Information integrity 4.8 116 [11] Tech-ethics 2.8 68 [12] Miscellaneous 2.5 60 *Some texts were tagged in more than one category.

In these reviews, farmers indicated that apps facilitate clear, ready-to-use knowledge, information and ideas regarding crop choices and cultivation, a timely reminder of farm action through crop calendar services and recommended fertilisers and pesticides according to soil and crop type. These helped young farmers kickstart new farming ventures, such as experimenting with a pilot farm and new types of crops. A few reviews suggested that ex-farmers returning after a break also used smart farming apps as an information channel. Farmers were appreciative that the latest technology and seed information, knowledge about government schemes and agriculture subsidies could be accessed in real time from these apps. Thus, as one of the example reviews noted, smart farming apps are positively disrupting the informational backwardness of rural agriculture.

Wow, amazing and beautiful application. [Name of app] works to fill the gap between farmers and accurate strategic agricultural information, with the involvement of agriculture experts. (Reviewer Agria1532) Among the advanced information services that the smart farming apps were found to facilitate were area-specific weather forecasting reports, mobile commerce for agriculture sale/purchase, real-time price data from markets (mandis), 4 and agri-doctor services via expert call/chat or through AI. Some of the reviews suggested that this provides good quality and, more importantly, timely expert advice. Apps like Plantix that utilise deep-learning AI systems give feedback about crop disease and recommend medicines in real time. Thus, apps disrupt traditional rural agriculture advisory methods that involve reaching out to semi-experts like pesticide shopkeepers and large holding farmers in day-to-day life. For instance, one of the reviews noted the following: . . . Very thankful to the [app name]. In India, shopkeepers will guide the farmers regarding the usage of fertilisers and pesticides. Farmers will blindly follow them. Now, everything has changed. Everybody is learning easily through online [apps]. This app is very useful. Once again, I want to thank you. . . . (Reviewer Plant24549)

The information on current market rates was also found to be empowering farmers, with a few reviews linking the use of smart farming apps with yield and income (increment) and cost (reduction). However, approximately a quarter of reviews concerned technical inadequacies, with farmers indicating that their lived experience was not meeting their expectations. We identified these from three categories of reviews: 'glitches' in advanced information services (7%), issues related to 'function, support and interface' (12%) and 'feature requests' (6%).

Farmers complained about delayed delivery in m-commerce, delayed expert responses to queries, discrepancies in weather forecasts and false-negative errors in automated image recognition. We also found farmers complaining about functional issues, such as glitches in registration and a one-time password-based login function. Such problems were significantly more prevalent in the government apps (e-NAM and Kisan Suvidha). Farmers also raised other specific technical issues related to user interface and server glitches. Through these reviews, farmers made a few specific feature requests, such as making app insights downloadable in pdf/audios/video formats for offline visits, which relates to connectivity and the rural-urban digital divide. Indeed, reviews were revealed as a systematic source of reflexive monitoring that can feed innovation designs:

Here are a few suggestions for developers 1. A search bar for the community questions as the stream of questions is steadily increasing. . . . 4. Clicking the photos shouldn't trigger the uploads immediately. Farms do not have good internet connectivity. . . (Reviewer Plant27274) It's a useful app for traders who buy commodities from mandis. There should be an option to delete rates quoted higher (wrongly quoted rates). No option has been provided for buyers to delete wrongly quoted rates A buyer only has options to increase the rates if quoted earlier. There's no option to rectify or delete the rates. (Reviewer e-NAM8192) Another primary concern, categorised under the theme 'landscape', concerned the inadequacy of the smart farming apps regarding advisory services tailored to specific locations, agro-climatic regions and crops. These apps have yet to enter the niche structure of India's cropping patterns and the farmers associated with niche cropping. Some 13% of the informative reviews suggested that farmers do not find app functionalities relevant for the specific crops, vegetables and fruit they were cultivating, while in 5% of the reviews, farmers complained that app information (agriculture news, government schemes, weather forecasts, etc.) were not location-specific. For instance, a farmer made the following requests:

Personalise the content and alerts after asking the geo-location and the crop the farmer is growing. Alert the farmers about pests and diseases spreading in their locality. Build a location-based chat community where farmers can interact about anything related to farming. (Reviewer Kisan10288) Again, there were issues with the government apps e-NAM and Kisan Suvidha, which were still not available in all states, thus raising questions about the nature of cooperative federalism in the context of digital transitions.

About one-fifth of the reviews indicated socioethical compatibility concerns. These were categorised as 'linguistic inclusivity' (10.8%), 'information integrity' (4.8%), 'tech-ethics' (2.8%) and 'miscellaneous issues' (2.5%).

Regarding linguistic inclusivity, farmers demanded app interfaces in their regional languages, including Bengali, Gujarati, Assamese, Tamil and Telegu. Farmers of nonhegemonic vernaculars-essentially, non-Hindi speakers, since the apps are generally provided just in the main official language of the country-found themselves linguistically excluded from these technologies. An interesting review to mention in this context involved the KrishiNetwork app, which is focused explicitly on the Hindi-speaking northern agro-climatic zone, which justifies its availability in the Hindi language-but not necessarily only Hindi. Thus, it might be considered linguistically majoritarian, insofar as it fails to include minorities and allow for anomalies. For example, a young farmers' mother tongue and language of practice may not always match as indicated in this (unedited) English-language review:

The moment we open the app its all in Hindi. Why English is not one of the choice to start with?? i don't have any bias, but fact remains that there could be several people like me who will find so difficult to read hindi because its been ages since i read hindi Eventhough it's my mother tongue. as of now i am deleting this app because its not feasible to take so much time reading. At the start on top there should be language option. (Reviewer Krish13749) Another systematic challenge is to make the use of these apps feasible for people who are unable to write (type) their concerns for accessing advisories. As this review notes Some people are not that literate enough to write their problems. If the use of a local dialect is available, then one can express his problem in his local dialect and there should be a translation into other languages. . . . (Reviewer Plant25155) Approaching one in twenty (4.8%) of the reviews suggested that the information flow from app to farmers was constrained by misinformation and disinformation, thus creating a question of 'informational integrity'. We define this in terms of a lack of trustworthiness in the knowledge, information and ideas flowing to the farming community via the apps. For example, market rates and weather forecasts not updated in real-time confuse farmers' decision-making, leading to losses. Inevitably, this will undermine confidence in and usage of the apps. Farmers also complained about the lack of adequate technical knowledge shown by the experts in the chat/call services offered. In a few apps, expert recommendations were not adequately explained to farmers, leading to fragmented trust in the information, ideas and advice being given. AI-based recommendations were also reported to lack proper explanation, with trust in them resulting in losses. Apps that support community networking for farmers to crowdsource information also led to confusion as different experts gave conflicting advice.
This review is representative of this type of problem:

Very good platform to look forward to in resolving problems; however, I feel there should be conclusive comment from [app name] experts in resolving the specific problem, over and above the responses of the community. That would give humongous confidence in the system that [app name] is intending to deliver. Nevertheless, [app name] is best!!! (Reviewer Plant25487)

Listed in the 'tech-ethics' category, 2.8% of reviews expressed data ownership and privacy concerns. These smart farming apps access phone data related to location, store content in the form of images, videos and files, keep contact data and read phone status and identity. Some reviews recognised the issue of privacy management and autonomy as the apps can only be used by sharing such data: Agree that you need to access our location for weather and commodity prices, but why do you need to access our phones call logs, SMS/MMS, files, accounts, email and attachments, camera etc.? (Reviewer Agria1738) Thus, we observed a utility-versus-privacy trade-off narrative in the reviews. Farmers were ready to share limited (e.g., GPS) data that could help better contextualise smart farming advisories, but they strongly criticised scraping everything from the phone.

Finally, some of the reviews categorised under 'miscellaneous' (2.5%) complained that emerging apps for digital transitions were built on the modern productivist principle. Hence, alternatives like organic and indigenous farming were excluded.

An interesting review to mention in this context involved the KrishiNetwork app, which is focused explicitly on the Hindi-speaking northern agro-climatic zone, which justifies its availability in the Hindi language-but not necessarily only Hindi. Thus, it might be considered linguistically majoritarian, insofar as it fails to include minorities and allow for anomalies. For example, a young farmers' mother tongue and language of practice may not always match as indicated in this (unedited) English-language review:

The moment we open the app its all in Hindi. Why English is not one of the choice to start with?? i don't have any bias, but fact remains that there could be several people like me who will find so difficult to read hindi because its been ages since i read hindi Eventhough it's my mother tongue. as of now i am deleting this app because its not feasible to take so much time reading. At the start on top there should be language option. (Reviewer Krish13749) Another systematic challenge is to make the use of these apps feasible for people who are unable to write (type) their concerns for accessing advisories. As this review notes Some people are not that literate enough to write their problems. If the use of a local dialect is available, then one can express his problem in his local dialect and there should be a translation into other languages. . . . (Reviewer Plant25155) Approaching one in twenty (4.8%) of the reviews suggested that the information flow from app to farmers was constrained by misinformation and disinformation, thus creating a question of 'informational integrity'. We define this in terms of a lack of trustworthiness in the knowledge, information and ideas flowing to the farming community via the apps. For example, market rates and weather forecasts not updated in real-time confuse farmers' decision-making, leading to losses. Inevitably, this will undermine confidence in and usage of the apps. Farmers also complained about the lack of adequate technical knowledge shown by the experts in the chat/call services offered. In a few apps, expert recommendations were not adequately explained to farmers, leading to fragmented trust in the information, ideas and advice being given. AI-based recommendations were also reported to lack proper explanation, with trust in them resulting in losses. Apps that support community networking for farmers to crowdsource information also led to confusion as different experts gave conflicting advice.

This review is representative of this type of problem:

Very good platform to look forward to in resolving problems; however, I feel there should be conclusive comment from [app name] experts in resolving the specific problem, over and above the responses of the community. That would give humongous confidence in the system that [app name] is intending to deliver. Nevertheless, [app name] is best!!! (Reviewer Plant25487)

Listed in the 'tech-ethics' category, 2.8% of reviews expressed data ownership and privacy concerns. These smart farming apps access phone data related to location, store content in the form of images, videos and files, keep contact data and read phone status and identity. Some reviews recognised the issue of privacy management and autonomy as the apps can only be used by sharing such data: Agree that you need to access our location for weather and commodity prices, but why do you need to access our phones call logs, SMS/MMS, files, accounts, email and attachments, camera etc.? (Reviewer Agria1738) Thus, we observed a utility-versus-privacy trade-off narrative in the reviews. Farmers were ready to share limited (e.g., GPS) data that could help better contextualise smart farming advisories, but they strongly criticised scraping everything from the phone.

Finally, some of the reviews categorised under 'miscellaneous' (2.5%) complained that emerging apps for digital transitions were built on the modern productivist principle. Hence, alternatives like organic and indigenous farming were excluded.

sections.paper_id: Kumar and Basu - 2022 - Can end‐user feedback inform ‘Responsibilisation’ of India's policy landscape for agri‐digital trans
paragraphs: The first theme emerging from the farmer feedback concerns technology as a positive disruption. As noted (above), a positive appraisal (∼44% reviews) of smart farming apps dominated the farmer feedback. Our findings have reaffirmed the positive role of digital agriculture platforms as illustrated in the literature (Cole & Fernando, 2012;Deichmann et al., 2016;Rose et al., 2016). Smart farming apps are emerging as a significant configuration for a digitally integrated and augmented advisory (Klerkx, 2021), with apps not only providing direct advice to farmers but also facilitating their social networking platforms for knowledge exchange, such as Krishify in India. These digital artefacts are rapidly reconceptualising agriculture knowledge innovation systems and their enablers and disablers (Fielke et al., 2019).

A common thematic thread among all the policy texts is the assertion of digital agriculture as a positive disruption of on-farm efficiency, farmer income and the work-life balance. Various texts in the Indian policy corpus (e.g., ICAR, 2018; MAFW, 2018a; NITI Aayog, 2018) posit digital technologies as having the potential to support farming improvements. These include better-informed F I G U R E 2 Emerging themes from end-user feedback decision-making (through apps, sensor data, etc.), reduced human workload and a better lifestyle for farmers (through labour-saving robotics, drones, etc.); reduced transaction costs, transparency and traceability in the supply chain (e.g., through IoT, AI, blockchain technologies); and low-cost equipment (e.g., 3D printing), a better understanding of climatic uncertainties (e.g., big data analytics in weather forecasting) and more effective agri-governance (e.g., drones for real-time crop estimates and apps to reduce claim settlement time in crop insurance). These productivist contours are further legitimised by digital agriculture's positive synergies with ecosystem services. In some recent texts (MAFW, 2018b; NITI Aayog, 2018), for instance, we find a pattern in the frequent appearance of words and phrases like 'yield', 'productivity', 'resource efficiency', 'income' and 'economic efficiency' followed by 'ecology', 'environment', 'sustainability', 'waste reduction' and 'conserving resources'. A similar meta-narrative is also found in the prime minister's speeches, with phrases like 'halving area consumption' (Gulati & Roy, 2017) in addition to the ubiquitous 'doubling farmers' income' (The Hindu, 2016).

However, the extent to which empirical evidence informs these positive disruption scenarios and claims (imaginings) is not clear. Rather, they seem to be riding on the techno-optimistic utility hypothesis and somewhat blinded by a globalising techno-utopia while the socioethical attributes of digital agriculture remain relatively undeveloped-something observed also in the European context (Barrett & Rose, 2022;Lajoie-O'Malley et al., 2020;Regan, 2019). Policy texts need to recognise and develop digital agriculture as a sociotechnical bundle of innovation. As Barrett et al. (2020, p. 4) note, 'identifying and bundling the right innovations is an intrinsically social process, one that demands cooperation, which is in shorter supply than are brilliant scientific insights'.

sections.paper_id: Kumar and Basu - 2022 - Can end‐user feedback inform ‘Responsibilisation’ of India's policy landscape for agri‐digital trans
paragraphs: The second theme emerging from the farmer feedback is the need for reflexivity and responsiveness. App reviews provide a rich repository of technical glitches and feature requests coming directly from farm users of technology (Gao et al., 2018;Iacob & Harrison, 2013). Thus, they can act as a systematic feedback loop for innovation coproduction. Mining such opinions, agri-tech firms and the government may instil reflexivity in technology design and make innovation more technically compatible with farmers' expectations. With the advent of machine learning, opinion mining has become a credible strategy for consumer-sensitive design (Genc-Nayebi & Abran, 2017). Thus, mining farmers' perceptions may also lead to increased value sensitivity, reflexivity and responsiveness in the design and development of digital agriculture platforms.

Embedding reflexivity and responsiveness are critical responsibilisation attributes of digital agriculture innovation. These dimensions, however, have only received token recognition in India's digital agriculture policy pathways. We illustrate a few instances here.

During the National Workshop on ICT Use in Agriculture 2014, the first of its kind, the need for knowledge coproduction focused on end-user feedback was underlined by the Secretary (Agriculture and Cooperation; MAFW, 2014). A call for a robust 'knowledge partnership' to drive interaction between farmers (and the public), the government and academia was also made to ensure that digital agriculture's initial trajectory remains socially reflexive. As the proceedings pointed out, . . . end product should be made simple and technology should be governed by end user feedback. The data created by one window of ICT should be available to all others. For instance, a large percentage of calls received in Kisan Call Centers relate to pests and diseases. This information should be made available to researchers, academia and even [the] common public. There ought to be enough dialogue with the State Governments to keep the efforts mainstreamed. (MAFW, 2014, p. 1) To some extent, we find a similar recognition of stakeholder needs in ICAR Vision 2050 (ICAR, 2015). As discussed in the last section, we find multilevel stakeholder engagement in the National Workshop on Application of AI in Agriculture (ICAR, 2018). MAFW's (2016b) draft report on smart farming app policy underlined the role of public participation, feedback mechanisms for app development and updated version releases. However, the mechanisms of stakeholder participation in and aiming towards reflexive and responsive digital agriculture are primarily informal. Legally instituted systematic and robust channels for stakeholder inclusion in downstream, midstream and upstream innovation are missing.

sections.paper_id: Kumar and Basu - 2022 - Can end‐user feedback inform ‘Responsibilisation’ of India's policy landscape for agri‐digital trans
paragraphs: The third theme emerging from the farmer feedback analysis concerned the design of inclusive technologies. The reviews suggested that existing smart farming apps are limited to very few crops, app interfaces do not include local languages and recommendations and advice supplied through the apps are only relevant to limited locations. These are antithetical to scale neutrality. While the adoption of farming apps in India may be neutral to land size and farmers' investment potential, they are not yet neutral to the end users' (farmers') vernacular, crop types and location specificity. Also, alternative farming systems remain excluded from the existing functional ecosystem of smart farming apps. There is a need for policy-level incentivisation and strong engagement with agri-tech entrepreneurs to ensure that digital artefacts engage with the diversity of end-users and their needs, requests, and suggestions from the very beginning at the formative stage itself. Agridigital configurations should be not only divisible (to meet scale neutrality) but also diverse (to meet end-user neutrality).

In the case of Indian agriculture, the inclusion of stakeholders is contingent upon the country's diverse socio-demographic farming characteristics as expressed in linguistic, crop, farm-size and digital access diversities. The Doubling Farmers' Income report asserts that digital agriculture's most significant challenge lies in articulating such diversities, which it thus recognises:

The challenges and opportunities from data analytics are immense in a country like India with 670,000 villages and 137 million farmers speaking around 800 languages [in] 127 agro-climatic zones capable of supporting 3,000 different crops and one million varieties. . . (MAFW, 2018, p. 147) In order to accommodate linguistic equity, under the Agriculture 2.0 mission mode project and later in the farming mobile app policy (MAFW, 2016a(MAFW, , 2016b)), states were given the responsibility to either create apps in English and vernaculars or to convert the centrally developed app into their local languages.

The scale neutrality of smart farming apps holds immense potential for marginal and small farmers, but the real challenge lies in enabling access, as they are commonly not digitally literate and aware. Efforts towards such a level of stakeholder engagement through digitised agriextension are missing in the Indian policy narrative. Another challenge lies in channelling smallmedium farmers' inclusion in investment-intensive configurations, such as GPS-and GIS-aided machinery, robotics and drones. This type of inclusion may require tailoring technologies according to farming diversities, for example, with low-power/low-cost equipment for small farms (Fleming et al., 2021;Lioutas & Charatsari, 2020). An example here is Microsoft's Strobe, a frugal adaption of existing smartphone Wi-Fi capacity to low-cost soil moisture and soil conductivity sensors for the developing world (Ding & Chandra, 2020). Envisioning around such frugality in designs of digital innovation remains limited in policy texts.

The quest for inclusiveness in digital agriculture in India is also fettered by conflicting imaginings emerging from different policy texts. Few of the texts underline the coexistence of small and large farms and hence the need for low-cost, small payload-friendly embodied technologies (ICAR, 2018). Some paint an overtly techno-utopian picture of the future in the form of larger farms (as a consequence of the contractual organisation of smallholders) run by smart entrepreneurs and smart computers (ICAR, 2015). 5 An inclusive digital agriculture transition in India is also contingent on recognition of and attendance in the digitally divided rural landscape, which will require a policy bridge between social and technological innovation (Klerkx & Rose, 2020). We find indications of such policy bridges in the Dalwai report. Policy imaginings recognise the role of village digitisation through last-mile connectivity, upskilling rural populations, creating a startup ecosystem, farmers' database creation and blending local knowledge with modern science and technology (MAFW, 2018).

Efforts towards such a level of stakeholder engagement through digitised agriextension are missing in the Indian policy narrative. Another challenge lies in channelling smallmedium farmers' inclusion in investment-intensive configurations, such as GPS-and GIS-aided machinery, robotics and drones. This type of inclusion may require tailoring technologies according to farming diversities, for example, with low-power/low-cost equipment for small farms (Fleming et al., 2021;Lioutas & Charatsari, 2020). An example here is Microsoft's Strobe, a frugal adaption of existing smartphone Wi-Fi capacity to low-cost soil moisture and soil conductivity sensors for the developing world (Ding & Chandra, 2020). Envisioning around such frugality in designs of digital innovation remains limited in policy texts.

The quest for inclusiveness in digital agriculture in India is also fettered by conflicting imaginings emerging from different policy texts. Few of the texts underline the coexistence of small and large farms and hence the need for low-cost, small payload-friendly embodied technologies (ICAR, 2018). Some paint an overtly techno-utopian picture of the future in the form of larger farms (as a consequence of the contractual organisation of smallholders) run by smart entrepreneurs and smart computers (ICAR, 2015). 5 An inclusive digital agriculture transition in India is also contingent on recognition of and attendance in the digitally divided rural landscape, which will require a policy bridge between social and technological innovation (Klerkx & Rose, 2020). We find indications of such policy bridges in the Dalwai report. Policy imaginings recognise the role of village digitisation through last-mile connectivity, upskilling rural populations, creating a startup ecosystem, farmers' database creation and blending local knowledge with modern science and technology (MAFW, 2018).
sections.preview_head: The third theme emerging from the farmer feedback analysis concerned the design of inclusive technologies. The reviews suggested that existing smart farming apps are limited to very few crops, app interfaces do not include local languages and recommendations and advice supplied through the apps are only relevant to limited locations. These are antithetical to scale neutrality.

paragraphs: The final theme to emerge from the farmer feedback involved the anticipation of socioethical risk. The feedback reviews outlined risks arising from information integrity concerns (∼4.8% reviews). While these apps give a sense of 'ontological security' to farmers-a feeling of being connected and plugged with real-time information and ideas coming from the experts-at the same time, misinformation, disinformation and contested information flows lead to 'decision dilemmas' (Chayko, 2014). Technologies must be designed to produce simple, actionable insights instead of bombarding farmers with a range of conflicting ideas (Klerkx & Rose, 2020). Facilitating information integrity will enhance farmers' trust in agri-digital artefacts, a significant socioethical implication for small and marginal farmers for whom the downside risk of misinformation (and the potential perception of such) is high (Fleming et al., 2021).

Additionally, some (∼2.4%) reviews outlined issues around privacy and data ownership. Scraping everything from a farmer's smartphone and then misappropriating the data gained becomes a significant socioethical challenge in an age of apps. Technology firms are better positioned to appropriate and financialise farm data benefits than farming stakeholders (Cowie et al., 2020;Fraser, 2021). Indian food sovereignty activist Vandana Shiva underlines the concern around new technologies reinforcing older power structures: First, they said without chemicals you can't grow food. Then, they said without GMOs you can't grow food. And now they are saying that without digital agriculture you can't grow food. (Vandana Shiva as quoted in Shah. 2021) Policy reflections around these issues become significant. The report on National Strategy on AI issued by NITI Aayog (2018) identifies AI as a critical configuration for digital agriculture and calls for 'responsible AI'. While AI applications reduce human biases in decision-making, an ethical bias may emerge through the engineer's initial value induction in the design and biases in the existing dataset. NITI Aayog's National Strategy recognises various socioethical concerns, such as policy for explainable AI and differential privacy and incentivising innovations principled upon privacy protection. There is a need to curate such deliberations from the farming space perspective. The mushrooming app culture in the farming space devoid of legally instituted policy to guide farmers' ownership and control rights over data and digital intellectual property may further reinforce class and labour inequalities and power asymmetry (Parra-López et al., 2021;Wolfert et al., 2017).

The anticipation of socioethical risks is not credibly instituted in other Indian policy texts. For instance, the Dalwai report provides 41 recommendations for modernising Indian agriculture, including the creation of farmer databases, such as a digital informatics network for farmers and private-public-partnership models for scaling, as well as the adoption of digital agriculture. However, uncertainties around uneven power relations emerging from farm data ownership between technology firms and the end users (farmers) have not yet been properly identified, let alone addressed (Bronson, 2018;Lioutas & Charatsari, 2020). The term 'privacy' does not occur even once in the 64 pages of the report that are dedicated to 'digital technologies'.

Information integrity issues are mentioned in a few of the texts, such as the MAFW (2016b) draft report. In particular, recognising the risks of information integrity while using smart farming apps strategises a mechanism to verify the content of freelance mobile apps. Additionally, in managing the challenge of informational integrity, the Dalwai report notes the following:

[T]here is no validation of the output that is arrived at by the various agencies functioning independently. This at times results in the information and output being at cross purposes and lacking integrity. This is a concern, especially in the extension services domain where the information touches the farmers, that needs to be addressed. (MAFW, 2018a) Embedding responsible innovation in policy-making will require policymakers to engage systematically on value concerns in design, including the institution of privacy and information integrity. In this regard, Bronson (2018) suggests that rights holders be involved through critical feedback workshops, open innovation and online feedback on upstream innovation policies. Imaginations and anticipations for a sustainable rescripting of agriculture must be ingrained with a balanced focus between the so-called 'hard impacts' (e.g., economic cost, impact on jobs) and 'soft impacts' (e.g., socioethical reflections) of digital agriculture (Parra-López et al., 2021;van der Burg et al., 2019).

sections.paper_id: Kumar and Basu - 2022 - Can end‐user feedback inform ‘Responsibilisation’ of India's policy landscape for agri‐digital trans
paragraphs: Returning to the title of this article, we ask whether end-user feedback can inform the responsibilisation of India's policy for the agri-digital transition. Based on the analysis presented, the answer is a resounding 'Yes, indeed, that is quite clearly possible'. However, it may be a little surprisingand certainly worrisome, given India's long familiarity with the IT sector and the push towards digitalisation coming from the top-that the revelation that the policies through which the transition has been imagined are truly quite inadequate and little thought through in terms of steering agri-digitalisation towards socially desired outcomes. This may be partly due to not knowing the desired outcome of a radical emerging innovation, such as digital agriculture.

Regarding the methodological contribution of this article, the main novelty lies in its analytical scheme reflecting upon RRI-centric policy-specific themes emerging from end-user feedback. We have shown that end-user feedback can be effectively analysed as a potential RRI activity to guide agri-tech innovation and the impending social and technological change. The scheme we have applied introduces topic modelling for policy research as a machine learning technique for content analysis. For large, textualised digital datasets, topic modelling affords an overview of hidden semantic structures and gives initial, value-neutral signals about emerging themes. Further, by validating topic-modelling analysis outcomes with qualitative content analysis, we argue that computationally deducted topics provide a sense of value-neutrality as a precursor to the qualitative coding of the texts.

The significance of this article goes beyond India. 6 Instituting responsibilisation in the innovation process through strong end-user centeredness may help innovation actors know and articulate what is desired (from the social or people perspective). This scheme of analysis may help researchers engaged in Europe's Horizon 2020's agri-digitalisation projects in tailoring, contextualisation and localisation of RRI into their respective agrarian societies. Specifically, our findings curate insights for global institutions driving food system transformation projects in the African Union and Asian countries (e.g., Food and Nutrition Security and Sustainable Agriculture (FNSSA) project-https://leap4fnssa.eu/, Horizon 2020's Enrich in Africa project-https: //enrich-in-africa-project.eu/). Even for global philanthro-capitalist organisations (e.g., Gates Foundation) invested in digital agriculture projects in Indian states and other Asian countries, our findings provide a sense of ground realities that should guide value inclusive technology design. Specific to India's policy landscape, emerging issues around farmers' data ownership and digital rights, the entry of tech giants and agri-tech startups, paradigms of linguistic and regional exclusions and concerns of small and marginal farmers all require urgent attention and comprehensive engagement at the level of policy-making. How much capacity India's knowledge and policy institutions have to do this. . . remains an open question! The sheer volume of data (socioeconomic, crop, biology, climatic, soil specific, etc.) generated, and its governance already represents a huge challenge.

Given the inevitability of agri-digitalisation, India may need both a comprehensive policy and a specific institutional architecture to manage the transition to a desirable one (i.e., to ensure the due consideration of all perspectives, actors, etc.). Perhaps agricultural knowledge institutions should lead the way and look beyond the ICAR research organisation to find (other) partners to create such an institutional architecture. In fact, India's entire AKIS structure needs a complete overhaul. Agri-digitalisation opens channels for a more contemporary multidirectional knowledge flow instead of the traditional, linear, top-down knowledge flow of the existing system. Technological front-ends fostering such overhaul need to be inclusive of nonhegemonic vernaculars, mindful of the digital divide, responsive to farmers' privacy concerns and focus on producing simple, actionable insights for farmers.
